intitule: Reading Comprehension: Understanding a Health Campaign Brochure

enonce.texte:
Read the text below carefully, then answer the questions that follow.


FIGHT THE FLU: PROTECT YOUR COMMUNITY

The seasonal flu is more than just a bad cold. It is a contagious respiratory illness caused by influenza viruses. Each year, it causes millions of illnesses, hundreds of thousands of hospitalizations, and thousands of deaths in our region alone. The most vulnerable are young children, elderly people, pregnant women, and those with chronic health conditions like asthma or diabetes.

However, the flu is largely preventable. The single most effective step you can take is to get your annual flu vaccine. The vaccine is updated every year to match the circulating viruses. It is not a live virus, so it cannot give you the flu. Instead, it helps your body build antibodies to fight the infection.

Beyond the vaccine, simple hygiene practices are your first line of defense. Wash your hands regularly with soap and water for at least 20 seconds. Avoid touching your eyes, nose, and mouth. If you are sick, stay home from work or school to avoid spreading the virus to others. Cover your coughs and sneezes with a tissue or your elbow, not your hands.

This year, our campaign, 'Flu-Free is the Way to Be!', is offering free vaccines at all district health centers every Tuesday and Thursday in October. Protect yourself, your family, and your entire community. Do not let the flu catch you off guard. Act now.
images: A simple, clear infographic poster for a flu prevention campaign. The main title is 'Stop the Flu!'. It shows three icons: 1. A syringe next to the word 'Vaccinate', 2. Soap and water next to the word 'Wash', 3. A person wearing a mask and staying home next to the word 'Stay Home'. The color scheme is blue, white, and green.

questions.texte: Identify the main purpose of the text.
questions.reponse: The main purpose of the text is to inform readers about the dangers of the flu and to persuade them to take preventive actions, especially getting vaccinated and practicing good hygiene.

questions.texte: List the two main methods of flu prevention mentioned in the brochure.
questions.reponse: The two main methods are 1) getting the annual flu vaccine and 2) practicing simple hygiene (handwashing, covering coughs, staying home when sick).

questions.texte: Explain why the text says the vaccine 'cannot give you the flu'.
questions.reponse: The text explains that the vaccine does not contain a live virus. Instead, it contains an inactivated or weakened virus that helps the body build antibodies to fight a future infection without causing the illness itself.

questions.texte: Analyze the statement: 'The most vulnerable are young children, elderly people, pregnant women, and those with chronic health conditions.' Why do you think the author included this specific information?
questions.reponse: The author included this information to create a sense of social responsibility and urgency. By identifying the most vulnerable groups, the author appeals to the reader's empathy and moral duty to protect not just themselves, but also the people around them who are at higher risk of serious complications from the flu.

questions.texte: Create a short slogan (maximum 8 words) for a new school-based flu prevention campaign, inspired by the text.
questions.reponse: Example of an acceptable slogan: 'Vaccinate, Wash, Stay Home: Stop the Flu Today!' (Answers will vary but must be original, related to the text, and follow the word limit).

intitule: Writing: Health Tips for a School Magazine

enonce.texte:
You are the health prefect at your school. Your principal has asked you to write a short article for the school magazine to raise awareness about a common health issue among students: auto-medication (self-medication).

Using the information from the lesson and your own ideas, write a list of 5 practical health tips for your fellow students. For each tip, explain the reason using a Type 1 conditional sentence (If... + will/can).

Your article should have a title and be written in a clear, instructive tone.

questions.texte: Write your article (a list of 5 tips with explanations) in the space provided.
questions.reponse:
**Title: Say No to Self-Medication: Smart Health Tips for Students**

Here are 5 essential tips to keep you healthy and safe:

1. **Always consult a professional.** If you feel sick for more than a day, you **can** visit the school nurse or a doctor for a correct diagnosis.
2. **Never share your prescription medicine.** If you share your antibiotics with a friend, it **can** be dangerous because their illness might be different from yours.
3. **Follow the recommended dosage.** If you take more than the recommended dose of a painkiller, you **will** risk serious damage to your liver or stomach.
4. **Read the leaflet carefully.** If you don't check the side effects and contraindications, you **may** have an allergic reaction without knowing why.
5. **Finish your full treatment.** If you stop taking your medicine as soon as you feel better, the infection **can** come back stronger and more resistant to treatment.

enonce.texte: Complete the following sentences using the correct form of the verbs in parentheses. These are all Type 1 conditional sentences (real possibilities).

questions.texte: If you (to take) ______ the vaccine, your body (to build) ______ antibodies.
She (to stay) ______ home from school tomorrow if she still (to have) ______ a fever.
If people (to not wash) ______ their hands regularly, they (to spread) ______ germs more easily.
Unless the government (to launch) ______ a new awareness campaign, many people (to continue) ______ to ignore the risks of auto-medication.
The doctor (to give) ______ you a prescription if you (to explain) ______ your symptoms clearly.

intitule: Vocabulary Building: Prevention and Health Campaigns

enonce.texte: Match each word from the lesson on the left with its correct definition on the right.

sousQuestions.reponse: d. A substance injected to help the body fight a specific disease.
e. Practices like handwashing that maintain health and prevent disease.
a. Physical signs of an illness (e.g., fever, cough, headache).
b. The action of stopping something bad from happening (like an illness).
c. Medical care given to a patient for an illness or injury.
